notion that Aeneas narration in Book 2 has the nature of a rhetorical self-

in the best possible light. An appreciation of this rhetoric can enhance our
appreciation of the subtlety of Vergils character presentation.

One of the inscriptions discussed by Escolà Tuset- from the Christian
necropolis of Torrebaja in Pueblonuevo del Guadiana (Badajoz) and
dating from the last third of the fourth century or first half of the fifth- is
an adaptation and imitation of a verse from Vergil. Gómez Pallarès writes
that the relationship between epigraphic texts and Vergil has always been
studied on the assumption that Vergils work (mainly the Aeneid) influenced
the inscriptions. But epigraphic literature written before Vergil influenced
his poetry in turn. Gómez Pallarès notes that the end of the Aeneid contains
specialized epigraphic vocabulary and argues that Turnus death is presented
as a mors immatura. Grandazzi s reexamination of unpublished notes from
the excavations of 1956-82 on the site of Augustus residence, as well as
evidence from new excavations on the Palatine, show that Aeneid 7.170-94
realistically describes the building where the Senate met. Hui compares the
leading of the defeated Carthaginians through Rome, in book 8 of Petrarchs

Fratantuono notices that the battle narrative at Valerius Flaccus 6.515-41 echoes Aeneid 11. Gellérfi writes that the walk around Rome in Aeneid 8 is not the only model for Caesar s (unhistoric) visit to the ruined Troy in Lucan, but also the katabasis in Aeneid 6. Hulls writes that both Statius and Silius Italicus shaped 124 - Shirley Werner Vergilian tradition in idiosyncratic ways, using metaphorical methods of looking back at Vergilian poetry. The basis for their reductive readings of Vergil can be found in the wider reading systems of the early imperial period, in particular those that involve Vergil as an educational text and those that involve systematized parody of Vergil. Maciver discusses the famous intertextual nexus evoked by a line in Quintus Smyrnaeus: at Quintus Smyrnaeus 14, Helen assuages Menelaus by stating that she left his home and bed unwillingly. Her words echo those of Aeneas at Aeneid 6.460; those of Medea at Apollonius Rhodius 4.1021-22; those of the coma Berenices at Catullus 66.39; and those of the coma Berenices in Callimachus, fr. 110 Pfeiffer. McCarthy reminds us that Vergils Aeneid and the Psalter attributed to David so influenced Augustines writing that the Confessions have been called a recapitulation of Vergilian epic in a Christian universe and also have been described as an amplified Psalter. Classicists and theologians have studied the individual influence of these works in Augustine s Confessions , but never in relation to each other. Augustine presents the Aeneid and the Psalms as embodying incompatible forms of life. For Augustine, Vergils epic produces a template for an imperial culture that institutionalizes a disordered drive to exalt oneself over others and to effect ones own will. The Psalms, by contrast, offer a therapeutic program to heal a disordered fixation on self and to help a person cling to God. Romani Mistretta notes that the phrase ingenti percussu amore in Georg. 2.476 alludes to Lucretius 1.922-25, mediated by Horac Epod. 11.1-2. Scafoglio 201 1 discusses the reception of Stesichorus in Vergil Scafoglio 2012 discusses a notorious inconsistency. At Aeneid 2.554-58, Vergil seems to follow a version of the death of Priam that differs from th 126 - Shirley Werner murder he has described at 2.547-53: Priams headless corpse is abandoned on the Trojan shore as if he was murdered there. It can be argued that Vergils possible allusion to the death of Pompey holds only a secondary, marginal meaning in the episode, which should be evaluated independently from historic allegory. Servius, Aeneid 2.557, invokes an unspecified tragedy by Pacuvius. In light of Vergils imitation, Servius comments on Aeneid 2.506 and 557 and Pacuvius, fr. 294 Schieri, can be connected with Pacuvius Hermionay and in particular to the messenger's speech about Neoptolemus death, which is the divine vengeance invoked by Vergils Priam. Scafoglio 2012 discusses a notorious inconsistency. At Aeneid 2.554-58,
Vergil seems to follow a version of the death of Priam that differs from th
126 - Shirley Werner
murder he has described at 2.547-53: Priams headless corpse is abandoned
on the Trojan shore as if he was murdered there. It can be argued that
Vergils possible allusion to the death of Pompey holds only a secondary,
marginal meaning in the episode, which should be evaluated independently
from historic allegory. Servius, Aeneid 2.557, invokes an unspecified tragedy
by Pacuvius. In light of Vergils imitation, Servius comments on Aeneid
2.506 and 557 and Pacuvius, fr. 294 Schieri, can be connected with Pacuvius
Hermionay and in particular to the messenger's speech about Neoptolemus
death, which is the divine vengeance invoked by Vergils Priam.
